design_philosophy.core_loop: Turn-based grid combat (7x7) with movement and skill execution, wrapped around narrative exploration with scenario-specific gimmicks
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Story-driven scenario variety vs. unified mechanical framework; each era presents unique narrative hooks (stealth, telepathy, scripted encounters) while maintaining consistent battle system
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Experience multiple protagonists across wildly different historical periods (prehistory to distant future), each with distinct narrative conflicts and personal stakes
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Radical commitment to narrative fragmentation—nine separate scenarios with different mechanics, character progressions, and battle encounter systems rather than cohesive world. Director Tokita prioritized telling multiple stories within one game, drawing each from different inspirational sources. Unifies through recurring antagonist Odio across timelines. No traditional RPG progression system; progression varies by scenario (some XP-based, some story-locked, some learned through observation)

difficulty.lives_system: Game over on player character/party defeat; no lives counter, per-scenario saves
difficulty.checkpointing: Per-scenario save structure; exploration and battles within scenarios progress continuously until party defeat
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Minimal difficulty selection mentioned; difficulty appears tuned per-scenario based on narrative needs rather than player choice. Turn-based grid system allows tactical repositioning and skill timing

progression.upgrade_system: Multiple progression systems: (1) Level-based with experience points unlocking abilities, (2) Story-event locked progression, (3) Learning techniques through observation of opponents. Equipment system with accessories for stat boosts and consumable items
progression.unlock_mechanism: Seven base scenarios freely selectable; completing all seven unlocks eighth scenario (Middle Ages chapter), which unlocks ninth scenario (final tie-in chapter revealing Odio across timelines)
replayability_hooks: Seven distinct protagonists with different mechanics and narratives encourage replaying scenarios
Scenario-specific gimmicks (stealth, telepathy, scripted encounters) vary gameplay experience
Learning enemy techniques requires observing different opponent actions
Different skill acquisition methods reward exploration of each scenario's unique systems

world_building_techniques: Scenario-specific visual and mechanical framing (prehistoric tribe dynamics, martial art lineage, ninja missions, gunslinger duels, etc.)
Period-specific character and narrative design—seven main scenarios each handled by different manga artists for visual distinctiveness
Recurring antagonist (Odio or name incorporating 'Odio') creates narrative connective tissue across temporally disconnected scenarios
Exploration environments themed to era (overworld, dungeons, town layouts)
Character-driven narratives with political/personal stakes (Sakamoto Ryōma in ninja scenario, tribe unification in prehistory, martial art succession in China)

boss_design.philosophy: Scenario-defining antagonists tied to narrative conflict rather than random difficulty encounters. Each scenario culminates in protagonist confronting powerful 'Odio' figure representing core thematic conflict
notable_bosses: Odo (Prehistory): Dinosaur god figure representing tribal conflict resolution
Ou Di Wan Lee (Imperial China): Rival martial school master embodying succession struggle
Ode Iou (Feudal Japan): Demon-daimyo representing external threat to Japan's opening
O. D. (Wild West): Bandit gang leader representing lawlessness in frontier town

faction_indicators: Tribal allegiances (Pogo's tribe vs. rival tribe, bonded through common enemy)
Martial school lineages (Earthen Heart vs. rival school)
Political factions (ninja loyalty vs. foreign occupation)
Outlaws vs. town law (bandit gang dynamics)
danger_signals: Sacrifice rituals and religious coercion (dinosaur god worship)
Succession violence and elimination of rivals (martial school killings)
Supernatural possession and deception (demon daimyo in human form)
Organized crime and gang territory control (Crazy Bunch occupation)
environmental_storytelling: Tribal gathering spaces and sacred grounds reflecting spiritual hierarchy
Martial training halls and dojos embodying school philosophy
Feudal castles and ninja compounds showing political power structures
Frontier town layout revealing economic control and lawlessness
